results.speaker: Female, Johannesburg, bilingual (Afrikaans/English)
results.text: This recording, South Africa 1, is copyright the International Dialects of English Archive. When the sunlight strikes raindrops in the air, they act as a prism and form a rainbow. The rainbow is a division of white light into many beautiful colours. These take the shape of a long round arc with its path high above and the two ends apparently beyond the horizon. There is, according to legend, a boiling pot of gold at one end. People look, but no one ever finds it. When a man looks for something beyond his reach, his friends say he's looking for the pot of gold at the end of the rainbow. I was born in also a rural environment, about 20 kilometers from the main city, in that particular area or angle, triangle of three cities, which were mainly industrialized cities. So it was fascinating to me as an individual growing up to be part of both an Afrikaans speaking background and going to Afrikaans speaking schools, in Afrikaans churches, but we were actually staying on the river. And the river traditionally has been the playground of the rich Johannesburgers, who were all mainly English. So I grew up until the age of five, not even in a certain sense hearing English, because I was at home and did not go to a kindergarten, then went to an Afrikaans school, and all of a sudden then came across all the English speaking kids on the bus going back and forth, and realized that there was actually another language being spoken in the country. Fortunately, some English speaking people from Britain moved in further down the road. They did not have children. And because my brother and I were the only kids around on the block, they obviously acted as babysitters. So eventually, then I got to understand what they were saying to me. And eventually, I think by the time I was six or seven, could distinguish the two languages. And that was almost the English influence, where the English influence started in my own life. It was always moving between these two languages and trying to understand the differences, because I think the language structure of Afrikaans is very much like German and Dutch. So it's a fairly, I think, a very uncomplicated language structure. And that's personally where I usually get confused, is my brain's working on both sorts of languages, and I sometimes get my language structure completely incorrect in both languages. And if I can do an Afrikaans translation of the sentence, Wanneer zonlig reendruppels in die lucht raak, wordt het een prisma in de vorm van een reenboog. So that's where the Afrikaans person would come from. The only other very, very clear Afrikaans dialect, literally, is again a group of Afrikaans speakers that stay in the Cape. And they have a very rolling R sound. So they would literally roll each R. So they literally, when they talk in R's, they roll their R's. So they would say, Wanneer wat strijks reendrups. It's literally that pronounced.

results.speaker: Female, 31, speech teacher, Cape Town
results.text: This recording, South Africa 7, is copyrighted by the International Dialects of English Archive. When the sunlight strikes the raindrops in the air, they act as a prism and form a rainbow. The rainbow is a division of white light into many beautiful colours. These take the shape of a long round arch with its path high above, and its two ends apparently beyond the horizon. There is, according to legend, a boiling pot of gold at one end. People look, but no one ever finds it. When a man looks for something beyond his reach, his friends say he is looking for the pot of gold at the end of the rainbow. South Africa is an amazing place, I think, and it has an incredibly vibrant spirit. I love living there. I know that the public image of Johannesburg, or the international image of Johannesburg, is one of fear, probably, of the crime rate, and of all the stories that seem to be publicized all the time, which tend to give quite a negative image. For myself, I find living in Johannesburg tremendously exciting and invigorating. I love the fact that there is such a vibrant mix of cultures. I love the fact that there are so many languages spoken in the place where I live, and I personally don't feel unsafe, although I know one does need to be careful. My accent is a Cape Town accent, and to American ears probably sounds quite British. In fact, there are many people in South Africa who speak the way I do, and a character that you might know would be Elsa from The Road to Mecca, Athol Fugard's play. There are a range of accents in South Africa. South African accents depend very much on, first of all, what your home language is, whether you speak English, Afrikaans, Zulu, Borsa, Tsonga, Sotho, South Sotho, North Sotho. There are 11 official languages. And then, secondly, where you come from in the country, and then, of course, factors such as socioeconomic conditions, the cultural groups that you move in, etc. All of those things influence the way we speak, as is the case in every other accent in the world. There is no one homogenous South African accent. There, in fact, would be probably no one standard. There are a range of standards, depending on whether you are a white speaker, a colored speaker, a black speaker, and, of course, what your home language is. The Afrikaans accent is probably the one which is considered to be typical. In all the movies that I've seen that have used, usually, bad actors to portray South Africans, they will have them doing bad South African accents, usually Afrikaans accents, and they will be incredibly heavy and over-exaggerated. Afrikaans accents also have a wide range within them, depending on the kind of area that the person comes from. There are rural accents, there are city accents, even suburbs. From one suburb to another, you will hear different sounds coming from people. The colored accent is a particularly interesting case and point, in that there are so many political issues around the whole idea of colored. In South Africa, some colored people consider the term colored to be offensive, and so would refer to themselves as being black. However, other colored people like the term colored, and, in fact, embrace it, because they see it as something which sets them apart from black people. Indeed, in South Africa, the colored community is a very homogenous community. And then there are other colored people who try and bridge this political debate and call themselves so-called colored, which is a way of compromising, I guess. So we have a lot of different colored accents. We have Indian accents as well. We have a large Indian population in South Africa, and for most Indians, English would be their home language, although they may also speak some Indian languages. And then, of course, we have the majority who are black speakers and come from all these different language groups.

results.speaker: Male, 18, Zulu background, Pretoria
results.text: This recording, South Africa 3, is copyright the International Dialects of English Archive. This recording, South Africa 3, is copyright the International Dialects of English Archive I did grade 8 there, so I had a problem with my grandmother. So my father said it's better to live with him, so I moved to Johannesburg. And I did my grade 9, yeah, grade 8. Then my grade 9 to the art school, National School of the Arts. And actually now I'm in it, currently I'm doing drama, studying drama. Yeah, and I'd say my father is also an actor, so sometimes we talk about acting. But when at school and they've given me assignments, I try actually to do by myself, actually to find if I'm really talented in this thing or what, you know. Actually to find things, find discoveries by myself. So I do that and then I come to school and present them, like find that okay, they're good. Then I start actually to have confidence in what I'm doing. Actually I don't have many friends where I'm living. But I like to go with my sister to the movies because I don't have friends. I actually have friends at school, but where I'm staying I don't. So yeah, I actually have friends where in Pumalang, where I grew up. Yeah, that's where I have friends. But sometimes I don't like to hang around with them because actually they're doing things which I don't like, like smoking, drinking, actually doing things which are out of the way. And actually I know what I want to do and I know what I want, so those things I know won't get me where I want to go. So yeah. Oh, my father. Actually he's involved in both theater and TV. So in theater, he's a resident director. And in TV, he's actually in a soap. Yeah, playing the main character, Archie, the womanizer. Yeah, so my father, he's a very, he's not strict, but actually he likes to operate with me openly, so I could do the same to him. So we talk about a lot of things, girls, everything. We talk about everything. So the relationship with my father is very, very close. We are actually in a cement. It's a cement. Yeah, so we talk about a lot of things. Oh, my dreams. Hey, I would like to be actually a movie director.

results.speaker: Male, 22, White English SA, Germiston
results.text: This recording, South Africa 4, is copyright the International Dialects of English Archive. When the sunlight strikes raindrops in the air they act as a prism and form a rainbow. The rainbow is a division of white light into many beautiful colours. These take the shape of a long round arch, with its path above high, and its two ends apparently beyond the horizon. There is, according to legend, a boiling pot at the one end. People look, but no one ever finds it. When a man looks for something beyond his reach, his friends say he's looking for the pot of gold at the end of the rainbow. People sitting around fireplaces, smoking cigarettes and drinking beer. I went to a nice sort of community-based school called Jermiston South Primary, where we participated in sports. We got a regular education sort of set for us, which was great. Found many friends, lots of water sports, going to Jermiston Lake, rowing, lots of swimming, lots of biking. I've lived with my family the whole of my life. My parents are still together. We're very strongly attached. I have an older brother whose name is Greg, and I have two adopted brothers who are Martin and Mark. I must be an outsider in my family because I decided to become an artist, but most of the people in my family are sports-orientated, which means that most of our time is spent on soccer fields with my brother kicking a soccer ball while everyone's parents were slowly but surely getting a little bit pie-eyed, which is drunk. Lots of jumping in rivers, lots of bicycle riding, as I've said, lots of letting other people's tires down. What we did for holidays was basically sit around and watch TV. We used to get a lot of videos, we, my father used to love nature conservation tape, so we used to watch a lot of that. Otherwise, just videos that have come out on the new circuit. We'd spend hours and hours in the swimming pool, oh, and making tree houses. We used to make huge, huge tree houses in the tree of our back garden, which was pretty amazing. Oh, and we also grew up with chickens and ducks. This is all coming back to me. Chickens and ducks. We used to have chickens and ducks in the back garden, which used to freak us out. We've had sheep in the back garden. You must understand, this is a very sort of community-based house, so we used to wake up with chickens and ducks cockle-doo-doo-dooing in the middle of the morning, which is quite fascinating. So we used to spend our holidays running after chickens, and my father used to try, he tried one day to cut a chicken's head off because he wanted to open a business with chickens, but it didn't work because he felt very sorry about cutting the chicken's head off because its body started running around the garden with no head. My mother, I must say my dad's disabled as well, I suppose. He's physically disabled, broke. He's back at work. My mother is the breadwinner in the family. She is loony. She's nuts. She's the one with the chickens and the ducks and who insists on having them.

results.speaker: Male, 18, Black, Johannesburg
results.text: This recording, South Africa 5, is copyright the International Dialects of English Archive. When the sunlight strikes raindrops in the air, they act as a prism and form a rainbow. The rainbow is a division of white light into many beautiful colors. These take the shape of a long round arch with its path high above and its two ends apparently beyond the horizon. There is, according to legend, a boiling pot of gold at one end. People look, but no one ever finds it. When a man looks for something beyond his reach, his friends say he is looking for a pot of gold at the end of the rainbow. Okay, I live in a family of four. Mother, father, sister, and me. I'm the youngest. I have a very vibrant family. Always energetic, always fighting, always laughing. Yeah, I have a nice family. It's nice. You know, I look at other people and I see they're divorced and I'm happy to have a family, you know. My mother and father are happy together, which makes me happy, you know. So, yeah. Okay, I live in Fleerhof. It's a place in Rotterdorf. Should I say South Africa? It's very vibrant. Streets aren't quiet at all. Every day of the week, you know. It's energetic. It's full, full of joy. It's, you know, everybody stands with one another, you know. Somebody else can come and say, okay, they look for trouble and they're not from there. Everybody stands up for their one person and says, okay, what's your problem, you know. Everybody stands up for each other in Fleerhof, so, yeah. Weekends, okay, lots of parties. Mainly, they would just drink and have a good time and make a noise and, you know. Yeah, not really a drug place, place for drugs. Yeah, it's not really violent. It's just nice. I do have extended family. Bloemfontein, Cape Town, Pietermaritzburg. Yeah, they come down often. We don't go there because they like it here. I always want to go down to Cape Town because I get a little bit, you know. I get sick of this place sometimes and I just want to go down to Cape Town. But, yeah, they like it here. A lot of holy, I have a holy family, so, yeah. Well, most of my uncles are pastors and, you know, poor pit, you know. You have to go by the rules. You can't get out of hand, you know. It's not like they beat you up or anything, but, yeah, they make you feel guilty, so, yeah. Okay, my mom works at Times Media in Rosebank, you know, for Sunday Times. She's financial bookkeeper for the section, for Sunday Times and Times Media. And she's very hardworking. She works till very late at night. I sometimes get very angry with her boss because, you know, I want my mother to be at home. And Rosebank is far from Fleerhof, you know, it's dangerous. My father, on the other hand, he has his own business. He is doing car spears at the moment, VW car spears. And he also likes it because sometimes it's a little bit rough, you know. Police come by and think that it's stolen goods and they raid. And it's exciting, but it's not that dangerous because we know we didn't steal anything, so, yeah. He likes his job. Well, I want to be a well-known producer, scriptwriter. And also one of my biggest dreams is to become a chef.
